text.title: Database design: PK type, ON DELETE, and agent antipatterns
text.summary: Global schema decisions (PK type, ON DELETE semantics), PostgreSQL-specific anti-patterns from LLM-generated DDL, and the human review checkpoints that must not be skipped.

rule.statement: Choose a single PK type globally (UUIDv7 for time-ordered inserts, BIGSERIAL for simplicity, ULID for lexicographic sortability) and document it in the schema constitution. Never mix PK types across tables — mixed types make joins ugly and type-mismatch FKs disable index usage.
rule.rationale: UUIDv4 random primary keys cause B-tree fragmentation on high-insert workloads. UUIDv7 preserves temporal ordering, matching the access pattern of most CRUD tables.

rule.statement: Specify ON DELETE explicitly for every FK. Never rely on the default (NO ACTION / RESTRICT — they are subtly different across PostgreSQL versions). Require human review for every CASCADE before merging.
rule.rationale: CASCADE silently deletes related rows. On a production database with 10+ years of data, an unexpected CASCADE can wipe terabytes. Review is the only safeguard.

list.item: CASCADE — child rows deleted with parent. Review required.
RESTRICT — prevents parent delete if children exist (hard error).
SET NULL — nullifies FK on parent delete (requires nullable column).
SET DEFAULT — sets FK to column default on parent delete.

antipattern.reason: The 255-byte limit is a MySQL pre-5.0.3 artifact with no meaning in PostgreSQL. Use TEXT with explicit CHECK constraints only when a length limit is a business requirement.
FK overhead is negligible for most workloads. Without a benchmark showing >10% impact, removing FKs silently degrades data integrity.

antipattern.description: Proposing materialized views without a refresh job and schedule.
antipattern.reason: A materialized view without refresh becomes stale immediately after creation. Require a documented refresh strategy (CONCURRENTLY + schedule) in the same PR.

antipattern.description: Audit trigger using current_user for the app-level user ID.
antipattern.reason: current_user is the database role (e.g., app_user), not the application user (e.g., user ID 42). Wire app-level identity via SET LOCAL app.user_id = $1 in the transaction.
